title: Understand: Slavery: An Overview
description: Check your understanding of the history of the institution of slavery in the United States with this set of multiple-choice questions.
topic_path: America’s Second Founding: Slavery, the Civil War, Emancipation, and Reconstruction

assessment_items.question_content:
**During the colonial period, slavery in America was \_\_\_\_.**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: outlawed by the Articles of Confederation
practiced in both northern and southern colonies
abolished in every northern colony
prohibited by order of the king of England

assessment_items.question_content:
**Although founders like George Mason, James Madison, and Thomas Jefferson expressed a desire to end slavery, \_\_\_\_.**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: they personally held many enslaved people and did not free all of them, even upon their own deaths
they saw no problem with the institution of slavery and encouraged the practice in new territories
they refused to take any concrete steps towards emancipation
they succeeded in eliminating in slavery only in Virginia

assessment_items.question_content:
**When discussing enslaved people, which founder admitted, “I am drawn along by the general inconvenience of living without them, I will not, I can not, justify it”?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Patrick Henry
George Mason
George Washington
Thomas Jefferson

assessment_items.question_content:
**A year after the Declaration of Independence was written, which man submitted a petition to the Massachusetts legislature, arguing for the abolition of slavery?**


[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: John Adams
Paul Revere
Prince Hall
Crispus Attucks

assessment_items.question_content:
**What was the effect of the Fugitive Slave Clause?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It protected those accused of escaping enslavement.
It provided land in the Northwest Territory for those escaping enslavement.
It allowed Southern slaveholders to retrieve enslaved people who had escaped to the North.
It declared everyone who lived north of the Mason-Dixon line to be free.

assessment_items.question_content:
The anti-slavery movement was part of America from the beginning. In January 1777, Prince Hall, a free African American in Boston, submitted a petition for freedom in Massachusetts, drawing on the principles of the Declaration of Independence. 

**By 1783, \_\_\_\_.**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Massachusetts had passed a stronger fugitive slave law
Boston voted to protect the slave trade
Massachusetts’s highest court had declared slavery unconstitutional
Paul Revere had told everyone about Prince Hall’s petition.

assessment_items.question_content:
**In his final public act, which framer and president of the Pennsylvania Anti-Slavery Society sent a petition to Congress calling for the abolition of slavery and an end to the slave trade?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Benjamin Franklin
George Washington
Thomas Jefferson
James Madison

assessment_items.question_content:
**The original Constitution of the United States protected the slave trade from abolition until what year?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

**Which statement best describes the reason for differing attitudes toward  slavery at the time of the Constitutional Convention?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Northern states were not as dependent on enslaved labor as southern states were.
Northern states had a higher population of enslaved people than southern states.
Southern states wanted a gradual end to slavery, while northern states never wanted slavery to end.
Southern states saw slavery as an issue of equality, while northern states saw slavery as an economic issue.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What did the southern states threaten to do if slavery was not protected in the Constitution?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Leave the Constitutional Convention and refuse to join the Union
Return to British rule
Vote to keep the Articles of Confederation
Replace the northern delegates

assessment_items.question_content:
One of  the most significant compromises at the Constitutional Convention was the Three-Fifths Compromise. 

**What was this compromise?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Enslaved individuals would be counted as three-fifths of a person for the purpose of congressional representation.
Southern states could keep three-fifths of their enslaved population but had to free the remaining two-fifths.
Only three-fifths of the states needed to agree to ratify the Constitution for it to become the law of the land.
For the United States to end the slave trade, three-fifths of states must vote in favor of ending it.

assessment_items.question_content:
**The language used in the Fugitive Slave Clause was taken from which other founding document?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Northwest Ordinance of 1787
The Articles of Confederation
The Declaration of Independence
The Bill of Rights
